title: Inga tetraphylla
taxonomy: Inga tetraphylla is a species of flowering plant in the family Fabaceae, commonly known as legumes. It belongs to the genus Inga, which comprises over 200 species. The genus Inga is part of the subfamily Caesalpinioideae, which is a large group of flowering plants. Inga tetraphylla is native to tropical America, where it grows as a small to medium-sized tree. The exact classification of Inga tetraphylla is as follows: Kingdom: Plantae, Clade: Angiosperms, Clade: Eudicots, Clade: Rosids, Order: Fabales, Family: Fabaceae, Genus: Inga, Species: I. tetraphylla.
uses: Inga tetraphylla is known to have various medicinal uses, primarily in traditional medicine. The plant's bark, leaves, and roots are utilized to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. Inga tetraphylla is also used to treat digestive issues, such as diarrhea and dysentery, due to its antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties. Additionally, the plant has been used to treat respiratory problems, such as coughs and colds. The medicinal properties of Inga tetraphylla are attributed to its high content of tannins and other bioactive compounds.

intro: Inga tetraphylla, also known as the "Guatemalan cedar" or "Quina", is a plant species native to Central and South America, belonging to the family Fabaceae. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, with reported health benefits including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antihelmintic properties, making it effective in treating various conditions such as fever, digestive issues, and parasitic infections. The bioactive constituents of Inga tetraphylla include flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made from Inga tetraphylla are infusions, decoctions, and tinctures, which are traditionally used in folk medicine to treat a range of ailments.
